1	**English** | [繁體中文](README.zh-TW.md)
2	
3	# OpenSpec GitFlow
4	
5	Turn AI coding assistants into structured engineering workflows — with schema-driven planning, checkpoint-based implementation, and full issue tracking on GitLab or GitHub.
6	
7	## What This Is
8	
9	This project is a **community extension** of [OpenSpec](https://github.com/Fission-AI/OpenSpec) — an open-source CLI by [Fission AI](https://github.com/Fission-AI) for managing change artifacts (proposals, specs, designs, tasks) in AI-assisted development workflows.
10	
11	OpenSpec provides the core CLI (`openspec init`, artifact pipeline, change lifecycle). **We built custom schemas and skills on top of it** to add:
12	
13	- **Issue tracking** — Parent/child issues created automatically on GitLab (`glab`) or GitHub (`gh`)
14	- **Checkpoint-based apply:** Executes one Task Group at a time, syncs closeout state, then pauses for review
15	- **Automated verify gate:** Runs lint, build, tests, and spec coverage before review — blocks review on failure
16	- **5-axis review:** Architecture, Security, Performance, Code Quality, Completeness — evidence-driven with explicit approve/reject/discuss
17	- **Progress sync** — Rich summaries posted to issues (objectives, completion, files produced)
18	- **Workflow labels** — State machine: `backlog → todo → in-progress → review → done`
19	- **Git worktree isolation** — Run multiple changes in parallel, each in its own worktree (opt-in)
20	- **Project-local installer** — OpenCode and Claude Code are supported in installer v1
21	- **Plugin distribution** — Install via Claude Code `/plugin install` or Codex `codex plugin install`. Symlink-based skill sharing from a single canonical source.
22	- **Composable skill hierarchy** — Skills are structured as Atoms → Molecules → Compounds with machine-readable metadata, validated by the `ds-skills` CLI
23	
24	```mermaid
25	flowchart LR
26	    A["/corgi-propose"] --> B["proposal.md\nspecs/\ndesign.md\ntasks.md"]
27	    B --> C["Issues\n(parent + children)"]
28	    C --> D["/corgi-apply"]
29	    D --> E{"Group done?"}
30	    E -->|Yes| F["/corgi-verify"]
31	    F --> G{"Pass?"}
32	    G -->|No| D
33	    G -->|Yes| H["/corgi-review"]
34	    H --> I{"Approved?"}
35	    I -->|Yes, more groups| D
36	    I -->|Rejected| J["Fix tasks added"]
37	    J --> D
38	    I -->|All done| K["/corgi-archive"]
39	```
40	
41	This diagram shows the command handoff points only. Inside that flow, `/corgi-propose` finishes by closing out into a tracked handoff state, `/corgi-apply` runs one Task Group through implementation and closeout before it stops, `/corgi-verify` runs automated verification (lint, build, tests, spec coverage) and blocks review if it fails, and `/corgi-review` gathers evidence before it asks for an explicit decision and applies the transition the user approved.
42	
43	## Quick Start
44	
45	### Prerequisites
46	
47	- **Node.js 18+**
48	- **An LLM Agent** — OpenCode, Claude Code, Cursor, AmpCode, etc.
49	- **glab CLI** ([install](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/cli)) — required for `gitlab-tracked`
50	- **gh CLI** ([install](https://cli.github.com/)) — required for `github-tracked`
51	- At least one of `glab` or `gh` is needed for issue-tracking features.
52	
53	### 1. Install to your project
54	
55	Choose one of three methods:
56	
57	#### A. Plugin Install (recommended for Claude Code / Codex)
58	
59	**Claude Code:**
60	
61	```text
62	/plugin marketplace add ricoyudog/openspec_gitflow_modified
63	/plugin install corgispec@corgispec
64	```
65	
66	**Codex**
67	
68	Open the target project in Codex — the repo-scoped marketplace (`.agents/plugins/marketplace.json`) auto-discovers CorgiSpec with `INSTALLED_BY_DEFAULT` policy. Alternatively:
69	
70	```text
71	codex plugin install corgispec
72	```
73	
74	> Plugin install creates symlinks from Codex skill paths to the canonical `.claude/skills/` directory. Skills update automatically — no sync tools needed.
75	
76	#### B. Bootstrap Install (recommended for OpenCode / any agent)
77	
78	Copy and paste the following prompt into your LLM Agent (OpenCode, Claude Code, Cursor, etc.):
79	
80	```text
81	Fetch and follow instructions from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ricoyudog/Coding_Corgi_flow/feat/openspec-llm-memory/.opencode/INSTALL.md
82	```
83	
84	The agent will clone the repo, build the CLI, and bootstrap your project automatically.
85	
86	> If you are using a branch or tag instead of `main`, replace `main` in that URL with the same checked-out ref.
87	
88	#### C. Legacy Manual Install
89	
90	For manual installation without an agent, see [Legacy manual install flow](#legacy-manual-install-flow).
91	
92	### 2. Review the bootstrap report
93	
94	Bootstrap writes `openspec/.corgi-install-report.md` in the target project and the agent should summarize whether it succeeded, stopped, or failed.
95	
96	### 3. Initialize cross-session memory (recommended)
97	
98	Open the **target project** in OpenCode or Claude Code and run:
99	
100	```text
101	# OpenCode
102	/corgi-memory-init
103	
104	# Claude Code
105	/corgi:memory-init
106	```
107	
108	This creates the `memory/` and `wiki/` directories so the agent retains context across sessions (what was done, pitfalls discovered, decisions made). You can skip this, but multi-session work will lose continuity.
109	
110	### 4. Start using the workflow
111	
112	```text
113	# OpenCode
114	/corgi-propose Add user authentication with JWT and refresh tokens
115	
116	# Claude Code
117	/corgi:propose Add user authentication with JWT and refresh tokens
118	```
119	
120	This generates all planning artifacts, writes the local tracked handoff state, and mirrors it to parent/child issues. After that, `/corgi-apply` runs one Task Group and its closeout, then stops for `/corgi-review`, which gathers evidence, asks for an explicit decision, and applies the transition the user approved. Then use the assistant-specific command form:
121	
122	- OpenCode: `/corgi-apply`, `/corgi-verify`, `/corgi-review`, `/corgi-archive`, `/corgi-explore`
123	- Claude Code: `/corgi:apply`, `/corgi:verify`, `/corgi:review`, `/corgi:archive`, `/corgi:explore`
124	
125	> **Platform detection**: All `/corgi-*` commands auto-detect GitLab or GitHub from your `config.yaml`. Same commands, either platform.
126	
127	## Install / Update / Verify Workflow
128	
129	Use the quick start above for new onboarding. The sections below are lower-level installer reference material for explicit install, update, verify-only, and legacy-migration cases.
130	
131	### Legacy manual install flow
132	
133	If you need to run the older explicit install flow instead of bootstrap, start here.
134	
135	### 1. Initialize OpenSpec in the target project
136	
137	```bash
138	cd /path/to/your-project
139	openspec init
140	```
141	
142	### 2. Run the installer from the cloned repo
143	
144	Open the cloned `Coding_Corgi_flow` repo in **OpenCode** or **Claude Code** and run the installer command.
145	
146	Examples:
147	
148	```text
149	# OpenCode
150	/corgi-install --mode fresh --path /path/to/your-project
151	
152	# Claude Code
153	/corgi:install --mode fresh --path /path/to/your-project
154	```
155	
156	If you omit flags, the installer prompts for the target path, schema, and whether to enable worktree isolation.
157	
158	The installer assumes the required user-level skills are already present. If bootstrap could not provision them, run `./install-skills.sh` from the cloned repo before retrying this manual path.
159	
160	### 3. Answer the installer prompts
161	
162	- **Target project path** — where `openspec init` already ran
163	- **Schema** — choose `gitlab-tracked` or `github-tracked`
164	- **Worktree isolation** — explicit opt-in only; never enabled automatically
165	
166	The installer copies only the project-local managed fileset into the target project:
167	
168	- `.opencode/commands/corgi-*.md`
169	- `.claude/commands/corgi/*.md`
170	- `openspec/schemas/{selected-schema}/**`
171	
172	It then patches only installer-owned keys in `openspec/config.yaml` and records the install state in:
173	
174	- `openspec/.corgi-install.json`
175	- `openspec/.corgi-install-report.md`
176	- `openspec/.corgi-backups/<timestamp>/` when a legacy install backup is needed
177	
178	### 4. Review the verification report
179	
180	Every install, update, and verify-only run writes `openspec/.corgi-install-report.md` in the target project.
181	
182	Review it before continuing. The report records:
183	
184	- prerequisite checks (`openspec`, `gh`, `glab`)
185	- user-level skill checks (`~/.claude/skills/corgispec-*`, `~/.config/opencode/skill/corgispec-*`)
186	- schema and `openspec/config.yaml` checks
187	- managed fileset sync results
188	- PASS/FAIL status
189	- whether any mutations were performed
190	
191	### 5. Configure additional project context (optional)
192	
193	The installer manages only the `schema` field and installer-owned `isolation` keys in `openspec/config.yaml`.
194	
195	You can then add project-specific `context` and `rules` manually:
196	
197	```yaml
198	# REQUIRED: which schema to use
199	schema: gitlab-tracked   # or github-tracked
200	
201	# RECOMMENDED: worktree isolation
202	# Each change gets its own git worktree + feature branch.
203	# propose/apply/review/archive all run inside the worktree.
204	# Without this, all changes share the same working directory.
205	isolation:
206	  mode: worktree           # worktree | none (default: none)
207	  root: .worktrees         # worktree root directory (default: .worktrees)
208	  branch_prefix: feat/     # feature branch prefix (default: feat/)
209	
210	# OPTIONAL: project context for AI
211	# Guides artifact generation (proposal, specs, design).
212	context: |
213	  Tech stack: TypeScript, Next.js 14, Prisma, PostgreSQL
214	  We use conventional commits and Prettier
215	  Domain: e-commerce platform
216	
217	# OPTIONAL: per-artifact rules
218	rules:
219	  proposal:
220	    - Keep proposals under 500 words
221	  tasks:
222	    - Max 2 hours per task
223	    - Each Task Group should be independently deployable
224	```
225	
226	> **Why enable worktree isolation?** Without it, all changes share your main checkout — you can only work on one change at a time, and code changes mix with your main branch. With worktrees, each change is isolated in its own directory with its own feature branch. On archive, the worktree is cleaned up but the branch is preserved for you to merge via MR/PR.
227	
228	The installer supports four explicit modes.
229	
230	### Fresh install
231	
232	Use this when the target project has no managed files and no `openspec/.corgi-install.json` manifest yet.
233	
234	- requires user-level `corgispec-*` skills to already exist under `~/.claude/skills/` and `~/.config/opencode/skill/`
235	- copies the managed fileset into project-local `.opencode/`, `.claude/`, and `openspec/schemas/`
236	- patches `openspec/config.yaml` minimally
237	- asks whether to enable worktree isolation
238	- writes `openspec/.corgi-install.json` and `openspec/.corgi-install-report.md`
239	
240	### Managed update
241	
242	Use this when the target project already has `openspec/.corgi-install.json`.
243	
244	```text
245	/corgi-install --mode update --path /path/to/your-project
246	```
247	
248	The installer compares the current managed files against the manifest hashes before updating.
249	
250	### Managed update with local modifications
251	
252	If the installer finds **local modifications** in a manifest-managed file, it does **not** overwrite it.
253	
254	Instead, it:
255	
256	- prints a diff
257	- stops the update
258	- writes FAIL status to `openspec/.corgi-install-report.md`
259	- asks you to resolve the local modifications manually before retrying
260	
261	### Verify-only
262	
263	Use verify-only when you want a health check without any file mutations:
264	
265	```text
266	/corgi-install --mode verify --path /path/to/your-project
267	```
268	
269	Verify-only checks prerequisites, user-level skill availability, managed fileset integrity, schema presence, and `openspec/config.yaml`, then writes `openspec/.corgi-install-report.md`.
270	
271	### Legacy install migration
272	
273	If managed files already exist but the target project has no `openspec/.corgi-install.json` manifest, the installer classifies the project as a **legacy install**.
274	
275	In that case it:
276	
277	- reports the legacy classification clearly
278	- creates `openspec/.corgi-backups/<timestamp>/`
279	- asks for explicit approval before migration
280	- aborts without overwriting if you decline
281	
282	For the full agent-executable validation scenarios covering fresh install, managed update, local modifications, verify-only, legacy install, and worktree prompting, see `.sisyphus/plans/corgi-install-smoke-matrix.md`.
283	
284	## Commands
285	
286	| Command | What it does |
287	|---------|-------------|
288	| OpenCode `/corgi-install` / Claude `/corgi:install` | Legacy/manual-only installer path for project-local asset install, update, or verify |
289	| OpenCode `/corgi-propose` / Claude `/corgi:propose` | Generate planning artifacts, then close out into tracked handoff state |
290	| OpenCode `/corgi-apply` / Claude `/corgi:apply` | Execute one Task Group, sync closeout state, then stop for review |
291	| OpenCode `/corgi-verify` / Claude `/corgi:verify` | Automated quality gate: lint, build, tests, spec coverage — blocks review if it fails |
292	| OpenCode `/corgi-review` / Claude `/corgi:review` | 5-axis review (Architecture, Security, Performance, Code Quality, Completeness), explicit decision, apply transition |
293	| OpenCode `/corgi-archive` / Claude `/corgi:archive` | Close all issues, sync delta specs, extract long-term knowledge, clean up |
294	| OpenCode `/corgi-explore` / Claude `/corgi:explore` | Thinking partner — explore ideas, check issue feedback, clarify requirements |
295	| OpenCode `/corgi-memory-init` / Claude `/corgi:memory-init` | Initialize the 3-layer memory structure (`memory/` + `wiki/`) for cross-session continuity |
296	| OpenCode `/corgi-migrate` / Claude `/corgi:migrate` | Import existing knowledge (docs, archived changes, vault pages) into memory/wiki |
297	| OpenCode `/corgi-lint` / Claude `/corgi:lint` | Validate memory health — freshness, size caps, broken links, extraction completeness |
298	| OpenCode `/corgi-ask` / Claude `/corgi:ask` | Answer questions from the vault using budget-aware retrieval |
299	
300	## Configuration
301	
302	All project-level config lives in `openspec/config.yaml`. The installer updates only the `schema` field and installer-owned `isolation` keys. Add `context` and `rules` after installation as needed.
303	
304	### Worktree Isolation (opt-in)
305	
306	Run multiple changes in parallel, each in its own git worktree:
307	
308	```yaml
309	isolation:
310	  mode: worktree    # worktree | none (default: none)
311	  root: .worktrees  # default: .worktrees
312	  branch_prefix: feat/  # default: feat/
313	```
314	
315	When enabled, `/corgi-propose` (OpenCode) or `/corgi:propose` (Claude Code) creates a worktree automatically. All subsequent commands (`apply`, `review`, `archive`) operate inside it. On archive, the worktree is cleaned up but the branch is preserved for you to merge.
316	
317	## Cross-Session Memory
318	
319	AI sessions are stateless by default. OpenSpec GitFlow adds a **3-layer memory system** — ≤2900 tokens at startup, self-compacting, Obsidian-compatible.
320	
321	```mermaid
322	flowchart LR
323	    subgraph "Layer 1: memory/ (always loaded)"
324	        A["MEMORY.md"] --- B["session-bridge.md"] --- C["pitfalls.md"]
325	    end
326	    subgraph "Layer 2: wiki/ (on-demand)"
327	        D["hot.md"] --- E["index.md"] --- F["patterns/ sessions/ decisions/ ..."]
328	    end
329	    subgraph "Layer 3: docs/ (untouched)"
330	        G["existing docs"]
331	    end
332	
333	    B -.->|"startup read"| D
334	    D -.->|"navigate"| E
335	    E -.->|"wikilinks"| F
336	    F -.->|"references"| G
337	```
338	
339	| Scenario | Command |
340	|----------|---------|
341	| New project bootstrap | Paste the Quick Start prompt into your agent — it runs `corgispec bootstrap` |
342	| Add memory to existing project | `/corgi-memory-init` |
343	| Migrate existing KB into memory | `/corgi-migrate` |
344	| Health check | `/corgi-lint` |
345	
346	**[Full documentation: Architecture, Lifecycle, Migration, Obsidian →](docs/cross-session-memory.md)**
347	
348	## Schemas
349	
350	A schema defines the artifact pipeline — what documents to create, in what order, and what the apply phase tracks.
351	
352	### Bundled Schemas
353	
354	Both `gitlab-tracked` and `github-tracked` produce the same 4-artifact pipeline:
355	
356	| Artifact | File | Description |
357	|----------|------|-------------|
358	| Proposal | `proposal.md` | Motivation, scope, capabilities, impact |
359	| Specs | `specs/<capability>/spec.md` | Formal requirements with WHEN/THEN scenarios (one per capability) |
360	| Design | `design.md` | Technical decisions, architecture, risks, trade-offs |
361	| Tasks | `tasks.md` | Numbered Task Groups with checkboxes — each group becomes a child issue |
362	
363	Pipeline: `proposal → specs → design → tasks → apply`
364	
365	Key design decisions:
366	- **Capabilities-driven specs** — The proposal declares capabilities; each becomes a separate spec file, creating a traceable contract.
367	- **Delta spec model** — Specs use ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED/RENAMED operations so changes accumulate into canonical specs over time.
368	- **Task Groups as checkpoint units** — Each `## N. Group Name` in `tasks.md` = one child issue, one apply session, one review cycle.
369	
370	### Creating a Custom Schema
371	
372	<details>
373	<summary>Expand</summary>
374	
375	Create a directory under `openspec/schemas/`:
376	
377	```
378	openspec/schemas/my-schema/
379	├── schema.yaml
380	└── templates/
381	    ├── proposal.md
382	    └── tasks.md
383	```
384	
385	Define `schema.yaml`:
386	
387	```yaml
388	name: my-schema
389	version: 1
390	description: Lightweight workflow with proposal and tasks
391	
392	artifacts:
393	  - id: proposal
394	    generates: proposal.md
395	    description: What and why
396	    template: proposal.md
397	    instruction: |
398	      Write the proposal explaining the change motivation and scope.
399	    requires: []
400	
401	  - id: tasks
402	    generates: tasks.md
403	    description: Implementation checklist
404	    template: tasks.md
405	    instruction: |
406	      Break implementation into numbered Task Groups with checkboxes.
407	    requires:
408	      - proposal
409	
410	apply:
411	  requires:
412	    - tasks
413	  tracks: tasks.md
414	  instruction: |
415	    Execute one Task Group at a time. Mark tasks as [x] when done.
416	```
417	
418	Then set `schema: my-schema` in your `config.yaml`.
419	
420	- `artifacts[].requires` — dependency ordering
421	- `artifacts[].template` — references a file under `templates/`
422	- `artifacts[].instruction` — tells the AI how to fill the template
423	- `apply.tracks` — which file contains task checkboxes
424	
425	</details>
426	
427	## How It Extends Vanilla OpenSpec
428	
429	| Capability | Vanilla OpenSpec | This Project |
430	|---|---|---|
431	| Issue tracking | None | Parent/child issues via `glab` or `gh` CLI |
432	| Apply behavior | Runs all tasks at once | Checkpoint-based: one group at a time, pauses for review |
433	| Progress sync | Local checkboxes only | Rich summaries posted to issues |
434	| Workflow labels | None | State machine: `backlog → todo → in-progress → review → done` |
435	| Review | None | 5-axis automated quality checks (Architecture, Security, Performance, Code Quality, Completeness) + verify gate + approve/reject/discuss + repair loop |
436	| Spec format | Generic | Delta operations (ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED/RENAMED) with formal scenarios |
437	| Worktree isolation | None | Opt-in parallel development via git worktrees |
438	| Cross-session memory | None | 3-layer memory system with ≤3000 token startup, self-compaction, and Obsidian compatibility |
439	| Knowledge migration | None | Guided import from docs, archived changes, agent configs, and vault pages |
440	| Memory health | None | 11-check lint (freshness, size caps, broken links, extraction completeness) |
441	| Skill architecture | Flat files | Composable 3-tier hierarchy (Atoms → Molecules → Compounds) with dependency graph, schema validation, and CLI tooling |
442	| Plugin marketplace | None | One-click install via Claude Code `/plugin install` or Codex `codex plugin install` with cross-platform marketplace manifests |
443	
444	## Repository Layout
445	
446	```
447	schemas/
448	└── skill-meta.schema.json          # JSON Schema for skill.meta.json validation
449	
450	tools/ds-skills/                    # CLI for skill validation and dependency graphing
451	├── package.json
452	├── bin/ds-skills.js                # CLI entry point
453	├── lib/
454	│   ├── loader.js                   # Discover and parse skill directories
455	│   ├── validate.js                 # Schema + constraint validation
456	│   ├── list.js                     # List skills with filters
457	│   └── graph.js                    # Dependency graph (mermaid/dot)
458	└── tests/
459	
460	docs/
461	└── superpowers/
462	    ├── articles/                       # Published articles and publish kits
463	    ├── plans/                          # Design and planning documents
464	    └── specs/                          # Feature design specs
465	
466	openspec/
467	├── config.yaml                     # Project-level configuration
468	├── schemas/
469	│   ├── gitlab-tracked/             # GitLab-integrated schema
470	│   │   ├── schema.yaml
471	│   │   └── templates/
472	│   └── github-tracked/             # GitHub-integrated schema
473	│       ├── schema.yaml
474	│       └── templates/
475	├── specs/                          # Accumulated specs (synced from archived changes)
476	└── changes/                        # Active change directories
477	
478	.opencode/
479	├── skills/corgispec-*/              # Source-of-truth skill definitions
480	│   ├── SKILL.md                    # AI-readable instructions
481	│   ├── skill.meta.json             # Machine-readable metadata (tier, deps, platform)
482	│   └── templates/                  # Template files (e.g., memory-init scaffolds)
483	└── commands/corgi-*.md              # Slash command dispatch
484	
485	.claude/
486	├── settings.json                     # Team auto-install config
487	├── skills/corgispec-*/              # Canonical skill source (physical body)
488	│   ├── SKILL.md
489	│   └── skill.meta.json
490	└── commands/corgi/                  # Claude slash command dispatch
491	
492	.claude-plugin/                       # Claude Code Plugin
493	├── plugin.json                       # Plugin manifest
494	└── marketplace.json                  # Cross-platform marketplace registry
495	
496	.codex-plugin/                        # Codex Plugin
497	└── plugin.json                       # Plugin manifest with interface block
498	
499	.codex/
500	└── skills/corgispec-*/              # Codex skill symlinks → .claude/skills/
501	    ├── SKILL.md
502	    └── skill.meta.json
503	```
504	
505	## Skill Architecture
506	
507	Skills follow a **composable 3-tier hierarchy** inspired by [Skill Graphs 2.0](https://x.com/shivsakhuja/status/2047124337191444844):
508	
509	| Tier | Description | Dependencies |
510	|------|-------------|-------------|
511	| **Atom** | Single reusable operation (e.g., resolve config, parse tasks) | None |
512	| **Molecule** | Workflow combining multiple atoms (e.g., propose, apply, review) | Atoms only |
513	| **Compound** | End-to-end orchestration combining molecules | Molecules only |
514	
515	Each skill has two files:
516	
517	- `SKILL.md` — AI-readable step-by-step instructions (what the agent reads)
518	- `skill.meta.json` — Machine-readable metadata: tier, dependencies, platform, version (what the CLI reads)
519	
520	### ds-skills CLI
521	
522	The `ds-skills` CLI validates skill structure and visualizes dependencies:
523	
524	```bash
525	cd tools/ds-skills && npm install
526	
527	# Validate all skills (schema + tier constraints + cycle detection)
528	node bin/ds-skills.js validate --path ../..
529	
530	# List skills with optional filters
531	node bin/ds-skills.js list --path ../..
532	node bin/ds-skills.js list --path ../.. --tier atom --platform github
533	
534	# Generate dependency graph
535	node bin/ds-skills.js graph --path ../..              # Mermaid format
536	node bin/ds-skills.js graph --path ../.. --format dot  # Graphviz format
537	
538	# Show dependency tree for a specific skill
539	node bin/ds-skills.js check-deps --path ../.. corgi-propose
540	```
541	
542	## Docs
543	
544	| Article | Language | Description |
545	|---------|----------|-------------|
546	| [Cross-Session Memory](docs/cross-session-memory.md) | EN / [中文](docs/cross-session-memory.zh-TW.md) | 3-layer memory architecture, lifecycle integration, migration guide |
547	| [OpenSpec 落地 GitHub](docs/superpowers/articles/2026-04-28-corgispec-github-workflow-zhihu.md) | 中文 | How we connected Spec, Issue, Review and Git workflow into a single pipeline — written for Zhihu |
548	
549	## Contributing
550	
551	1. Fork and clone this repo
552	2. Create or update a skill folder under `.opencode/skills/`
553	3. Each skill needs:
554	   - `SKILL.md` with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`) — AI instructions
555	   - `skill.meta.json` conforming to `schemas/skill-meta.schema.json` — metadata for the CLI
556	4. Run `node tools/ds-skills/bin/ds-skills.js validate --path .` to check your changes
557	5. Test locally before submitting a PR
558	6. Supporting files go in `references/`, `agents/`, or `templates/` subdirectories
559	
560	## Acknowledgments
561	
562	This project is built on top of [OpenSpec](https://github.com/Fission-AI/OpenSpec) by [Fission AI](https://github.com/Fission-AI). The core CLI, artifact pipeline engine, and change lifecycle management are all provided by OpenSpec — we extend it with custom schemas, AI skills, and issue-tracking integrations.
563	
564	If you find this useful, please also star the [original OpenSpec repo](https://github.com/Fission-AI/OpenSpec).
565

1	# AGENTS.md
2	
3	## What this repo is
4	
5	A workflow/skills toolkit (not an app) that turns AI coding assistants into structured engineering workflows via OpenSpec. It ships skills, schemas, CLI tooling, and slash commands for Claude Code, OpenCode, and Codex.
6	
7	There is no application code to build or deploy. The "product" is the skill files and their metadata.
8	
9	## Directory layout
10	
11	| Path | Role |
12	|------|------|
13	| `.opencode/skills/corgispec-*/` | **Source of truth** for skill definitions (SKILL.md + skill.meta.json) |
14	| `.opencode/commands/corgi-*.md` | OpenCode slash command dispatch |
15	| `.claude/skills/corgispec-*/` | Claude Code skill mirrors — must stay in sync with `.opencode/skills/` |
16	| `.codex/skills/corgispec-*/` | Codex skill mirrors — must stay in sync with `.opencode/skills/` |
17	| `openspec/schemas/` | Schema definitions (gitlab-tracked, github-tracked) |
18	| `openspec/config.yaml` | Project-level config (schema, isolation, context) |
19	| `schemas/skill-meta.schema.json` | JSON Schema for validating `skill.meta.json` |
20	| `packages/corgispec/` | **corgispec CLI** — unified CLI replacing ds-skills + install-skills.sh |
21	| `tools/ds-skills/` | Node CLI for skill validation + dependency graphing (legacy, use corgispec instead) |
22	| `install-skills.sh` | Installs user-level skills (legacy, use `corgispec install` instead) |
23	
24	## Commands
25	
26	### Run corgispec tests
27	
28	```bash
29	cd packages/corgispec && npm test
30	```
31	
32	Uses Vitest. Requires Node >= 18.
33	
34	### Validate all skills (the closest thing to a build/lint step)
35	
36	```bash
37	cd tools/ds-skills && npm install && node bin/ds-skills.js validate --path ../..
38	```
39	
40	### Run ds-skills tests
41	
42	```bash
43	cd tools/ds-skills && npm test
44	```
45	
46	Uses Node.js built-in test runner (`node --test`). Requires Node >= 18.
47	
48	### Other ds-skills commands
49	
50	```bash
51	node bin/ds-skills.js list --path ../..    # list all skills
52	node bin/ds-skills.js graph --path ../..   # dependency graph
53	```
54	
55	### Install skills to user level
56	
57	```bash
58	./install-skills.sh          # copies to ~/.claude/skills/ and ~/.config/opencode/skill/
59	./install-skills.sh --dry-run  # preview only
60	```
61	
62	## Skill authoring rules
63	
64	- Each skill lives in its own directory with exactly two files: `SKILL.md` (instructions) and `skill.meta.json` (metadata).
65	- `skill.meta.json` must validate against `schemas/skill-meta.schema.json`.
66	- Skill slugs are kebab-case, must match the directory name.
67	- Tier hierarchy: `atom` (no deps) -> `molecule` (depends on atoms) -> `compound` (depends on molecules/atoms).
68	- Platform field: `universal`, `github`, or `gitlab`.
69	- `corgispec-gh-*` skills are GitHub variants; unprefixed `corgispec-*` are GitLab or universal.
70	
71	## Three-directory sync obligation
72	
73	`.opencode/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`, and `.codex/skills/` must contain equivalent skill content. When editing a skill, update all three directories. The `.opencode/skills/` version is canonical.
74	
75	## No CI
76	
77	There are no CI workflows. Validation is manual via `ds-skills validate` or `corgispec validate`.
78	
79	## Config
80	
81	- `openspec/config.yaml` controls which schema (`gitlab-tracked` or `github-tracked`) is active and optional worktree isolation settings.
82	- `.opencode/package.json` only declares `@opencode-ai/plugin` — this enables skill discovery in OpenCode sessions.
83	
84	## Conventions
85	
86	- Markdown skill files reference tool names generically. Platform-specific tool mapping is handled at runtime by each AI platform.
87	- The workflow is checkpoint-based: propose -> apply (one Task Group at a time) -> review -> archive.
88	- Delta specs use ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED/RENAMED operations.
89	
90	## Coding Guidelines
91	
92	Behavioral guidelines to reduce common LLM coding mistakes. Merge with project-specific instructions as needed.
93	
94	> **Tradeoff:** These guidelines bias toward caution over speed. For trivial tasks, use judgment.
95	
96	### 1. Think Before Coding
97	
98	Don't assume. Don't hide confusion. Surface tradeoffs.
99	
100	Before implementing:
101	- State your assumptions explicitly. If uncertain, ask.
102	- If multiple interpretations exist, present them — don't pick silently.
103	- If a simpler approach exists, say so. Push back when warranted.
104	- If something is unclear, stop. Name what's confusing. Ask.
105	
106	### 2. Simplicity First
107	
108	Minimum code that solves the problem. Nothing speculative.
109	
110	- No features beyond what was asked.
111	- No abstractions for single-use code.
112	- No "flexibility" or "configurability" that wasn't requested.
113	- No error handling for impossible scenarios.
114	- If you write 200 lines and it could be 50, rewrite it.
115	
116	Ask yourself: *"Would a senior engineer say this is overcomplicated?"* If yes, simplify.
117	
118	### 3. Surgical Changes
119	
120	Touch only what you must. Clean up only your own mess.
121	
122	When editing existing code:
123	- Don't "improve" adjacent code, comments, or formatting.
124	- Don't refactor things that aren't broken.
125	- Match existing style, even if you'd do it differently.
126	- If you notice unrelated dead code, mention it — don't delete it.
127	
128	When your changes create orphans:
129	- Remove imports/variables/functions that YOUR changes made unused.
130	- Don't remove pre-existing dead code unless asked.
131	
132	**The test:** Every changed line should trace directly to the user's request.
133	
134	### 4. Goal-Driven Execution
135	
136	Define success criteria. Loop until verified.
137	
138	Transform tasks into verifiable goals:
139	- "Add validation" → "Write tests for invalid inputs, then make them pass"
140	- "Fix the bug" → "Write a test that reproduces it, then make it pass"
141	- "Refactor X" → "Ensure tests pass before and after"
142	
143	For multi-step tasks, state a brief plan:
144	```
145	1. [Step] → verify: [check]
146	2. [Step] → verify: [check]
147	3. [Step] → verify: [check]
148	```
149	
150	Strong success criteria let you loop independently. Weak criteria ("make it work") require constant clarification.
151	
152	---
153	
154	*These guidelines are working if: fewer unnecessary changes in diffs, fewer rewrites due to overcomplication, and clarifying questions come before implementation rather than after mistakes.*
155	
156	## Session Memory Protocol
157	
158	### Startup (every session)
159	Read in order, max 3 files:
160	1. `memory/session-bridge.md` — last session's state
161	2. `wiki/hot.md` — current project context (~500 words, hard cap 600)
162	3. `wiki/index.md` — jump to relevant domain page
163	Then read `docs/` or code as needed.
164	
165	### Retrieval Budget
166	- Startup: max 3 files (session-bridge + hot + index), then on-demand
167	- Per-question: max 2 wiki pages before answering
168	- If >5 pages needed: say "this needs a deep session"
169	
170	### File Size Limits (hard caps)
171	| File | Target | Hard Cap | Overflow Action |
172	|------|--------|----------|-----------------|
173	| wiki/hot.md | 500 words | 600 words | Trim oldest entries |
174	| wiki/index.md | 40 lines | 80 lines | Archive completed entries |
175	| memory/pitfalls.md | 10 active | 20 active | Rotate oldest 10 |
176	| memory/session-bridge.md | 30 lines | 50 lines | Archive old Done items |
177	
178	### Shutdown (every session end)
179	Update `memory/session-bridge.md`: Done / Waiting / New Pitfalls / New Discoveries
180	
181	### corgi Apply -> Long-term Memory
182	After each Task Group completes:
183	- New pitfalls -> append to `memory/pitfalls.md` (link source change)
184	- New implicit rules -> append to `wiki/architecture/implicit-contracts.md`
185	- Update `wiki/hot.md` Recent Decisions
186	
187	### Compaction Triggers (agent self-maintains)
188	- Every archive: compress session-bridge
189	- pitfalls > 20 entries: rotate oldest 10 to Archive section
190	- hot.md > 550 words: trim oldest entries
191	- Every 10 corgi sessions: suggest running /corgi-lint
192

11	
12	
13	      Sections:
14	
15	      - **Why**: 1-2 sentences on the problem or opportunity. What problem does
16	      this solve? Why now?
17	
18	      - **What Changes**: Bullet list of changes. Be specific about new
19	      capabilities, modifications, or removals. Mark breaking changes with
20	      **BREAKING**.
21

23	        - **New Capabilities**: List capabilities being introduced. Each becomes a new `specs/<name>/spec.md`. Use kebab-case names (e.g., `user-auth`, `data-export`).
24	        - **Modified Capabilities**: List existing capabilities whose REQUIREMENTS are changing. Only include if spec-level behavior changes (not just implementation details). Each needs a delta spec file. Check `openspec/specs/` for existing spec names. Leave empty if no requirement changes.
25	      - **Impact**: Affected code, APIs, dependencies, or systems.

27	
28	      IMPORTANT: The Capabilities section is critical. It creates the contract
29	      between

182	      Include alternatives considered for each decision.
183	
184	      - **Risks / Trade-offs**: Known limitations, things that could go wrong.
185	      Format: [Risk] → Mitigation
186	
187	      - **Data Model** (if applicable): ALWAYS include this heading.
188	      When the change involves schema changes, new entities, field definitions,
189	      or relationship changes, describe the data model clearly enough for
190	      implementation. If not applicable, keep the heading and write
191	      "Not applicable — no data model changes in this change."
192	
193	      - **API Contracts** (if applicable): ALWAYS include this heading.
194	      When the change involves new or modified API endpoints, document
195	      request/response shapes and note any breaking changes. If not applicable,
196	      keep the heading and write "Not applicable — no API surface changes in
197	      this change."
198	
199	      - **Migration Plan**: Steps to deploy, rollback strategy (if applicable)
200	
201	      - **Open Questions**: Outstanding decisions or unknowns to resolve
202	
203	
204	      Focus on architecture and approach, not line-by-line implementation.
205	
206	      Reference the proposal for motivation and specs for requirements.
207	
208	
209	      Good design docs explain the "why" behind technical decisions.
210	    requires:
211	      - proposal
212	  - id: tasks
213	    generates: tasks.md
214	    description: Implementation checklist with trackable tasks
215	    template: tasks.md
216	    instruction: >
217	      Create the task list that breaks down the implementation work.
218	
219	
220	      **IMPORTANT: Follow the template below exactly.** The apply phase parses
221	
222	      checkbox format to track progress. Tasks not using `- [ ]` won't be
223	      tracked.
224	
225	
226	      IMPORTANT: Each ## numbered heading defines a Task Group.
227	      Task Groups are checkpoint units — the apply phase executes one group at a
228	      time and pauses between groups. Each Task Group will become a separate
229	      GitLab child issue for independent tracking and review.
230	
231	      Design groups to be:
232	      - Completable in a single session
233	      - Independently reviewable
234	      - Self-contained (clear objectives per group)
235	
236	
237	      Guidelines:
238	
239	      - Group related tasks under ## numbered headings
240	
241	      - Each task MUST be a checkbox: `- [ ] X.Y Task description`
242	
243	      - Tasks should be small enough to complete in one session
244	
245	      - Order tasks by dependency (what must be done first?)
246	
247	
248	      Example:
249	
250	      ```
251	
252	      ## 1. Setup
253	
254	
255	      - [ ] 1.1 Create new module structure
256	
257	      - [ ] 1.2 Add dependencies to package.json
258	
259	
260	      ## 2. Core Implementation
261	
262	
263	      - [ ] 2.1 Implement data export function
264	
265	      - [ ] 2.2 Add CSV formatting utilities
266	
267	      ```
268	
269	
270	      Reference specs for what needs to be built, design for how to build it.
271	
272	      Each task should be verifiable - you know when it's done.
273	    requires:
274	      - specs
275	      - design
276	apply:
277	  requires:
278	    - tasks
279	  tracks: tasks.md
280	  instruction: |
281	    Execute one Task Group at a time. After completing all tasks in a group:
282	    1. Mark all group tasks as [x] in tasks.md
283	    2. Sync progress to the group's child GitLab issue (if tracked)
284	    3. Pause and report progress to the user
285	
286	    Resume on next /apply invocation to execute the next group.
287	    Pause if you hit blockers or need clarification.

7	
8	# Three-Layer Memory Architecture
9	
10	## Context
11	AI coding sessions are stateless. Each new session re-discovers context, wastes tokens, and repeats mistakes. Projects need cross-session continuity without external databases or runtime dependencies.
12	
13	## Pattern
14	Separate memory into three layers with different lifecycles and access patterns:
15	
16	1. **Permanent layer** (`memory/`) — Always loaded at startup. Contains project identity (MEMORY.md), session handoff state (session-bridge.md), and pitfall log (pitfalls.md). Budget: ~1300 tokens.
17	2. **Long-term wiki** (`wiki/`) — Loaded on demand via wikilinks. Contains patterns, session summaries, decisions, research, questions. Organized by subdirectory. Navigated via `hot.md` (project pulse) and `index.md` (hub).
18	3. **Raw docs** (`docs/`) — Existing human-authored documentation. Never modified by memory system. Referenced as needed.
19	
20	Startup reads exactly 3 files: session-bridge + hot.md + index.md. Total budget: ~2900 tokens.
21	
22	## When to Use
23	- Project has multiple AI-assisted sessions over weeks/months
24	- Need to preserve decisions, pitfalls, and progress across sessions
25	- Obsidian-compatible vault structure is desired
26	- No external runtime or database is available
27	
28	## Example
29	From the openspec-llm-memory change: `memory/session-bridge.md` carries the active change name, phase, branch, and last commit. On startup, the AI reads this first and immediately knows where to resume. `wiki/hot.md` provides the 500-word project pulse. `wiki/index.md` links to domain-specific pages for deeper dives.
30	
31	## Source
32	- Extracted from change: [[openspec/changes/archive/2026-05-05-openspec-llm-memory/proposal]]
33	- Design rationale: [[openspec/changes/archive/2026-05-05-openspec-llm-memory/design]] (Decision #1)

7	
8	# Self-Compacting Memory with Word/Line Caps
9	
10	## Context
11	Memory files grow unboundedly if not managed. Without a runtime process, there is no cron job or daemon to compact files. The AI agent is the only writer.
12	
13	## Pattern
14	Encode hard size caps directly in the agent's instructions (AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md Session Memory Protocol). The AI self-enforces during every write:
15	
16	| File | Target | Hard Cap | Overflow Action |
17	|------|--------|----------|-----------------|
18	| wiki/hot.md | 500 words | 600 words | Trim oldest entries |
19	| wiki/index.md | 40 lines | 80 lines | Archive completed entries |
20	| memory/pitfalls.md | 10 active | 20 active | Rotate oldest 10 to Archive |
21	| memory/session-bridge.md | 30 lines | 50 lines | Archive old Done items |
22	
23	A separate lint skill validates post-hoc that caps are respected, catching any self-enforcement failures.
24	
25	## When to Use
26	- Pure-instruction AI systems with no runtime
27	- Any memory/log file that grows with each session
28	- Word counts serve as reliable token proxies (1 word ~ 1.3 English tokens)
29	
30	## Example
31	From openspec-llm-memory: `wiki/hot.md` has a compaction trigger at 550 words. When the AI finishes writing, it counts words. If > 550, it trims the oldest "Recently Shipped" entries to stay under 600. The lint skill's check #9 catches any file that exceeded the cap.
32	
33	## Source

35	- Design rationale: [[openspec/changes/archive/2026-05-05-openspec-llm-memory/design]] (Decision #2, #5)

6	
7	# Architecture & Design Overview
8	
9	> Comprehensive analysis of the OpenSpec GitFlow (Coding Corgi Flow) project — structure, design decisions, and key abstractions.
10	
11	## 1. Project Identity
12	
13	This is **not an application** but a **workflow/skills toolkit** built on top of [OpenSpec](https://github.com/Fission-AI/OpenSpec) by Fission AI. It turns LLM Agents into structured engineering workflow engines via custom schemas, AI skills, slash commands, and issue-tracking integrations.
14	
15	**Supported AI platforms**: OpenCode, Claude Code, Codex.
16	
17	## 2. Core Workflow Pipeline
18	
19	```
20	propose → apply (one Task Group) → verify → review → (loop or archive)
21	```
22	
23	| Phase | What happens |
24	|-------|-------------|
25	| **Propose** | Generate proposal.md, specs/, design.md, tasks.md; create parent/child issues |
26	| **Apply** | Execute one Task Group at a time; mark checkboxes; sync to child issue; pause |
27	| **Verify** | Automated gate: lint, build, tests, spec coverage — blocks review on failure |
28	| **Review** | 5-axis quality check (Architecture, Security, Performance, Code Quality, Completeness); explicit approve/reject/discuss |
29	| **Archive** | Close issues, sync delta specs into canonical specs, extract knowledge, clean up |
30	
31	### Key Design Decisions
32	
33	- **Checkpoint-based apply**: One Task Group per session, enforced pause between groups
34	- **Delta spec model**: ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED/RENAMED operations accumulate into canonical specs
35	- **Parent/child issues**: One parent issue + one child per Task Group
36	- **Capabilities-driven specs**: Proposal declares capabilities; each becomes a separate spec file
37	- **Worktree isolation** (opt-in): Parallel development via `git worktree`
38	
39	## 3. Directory Layout
40	
41	```
42	Coding_Corgi_flow/
43	├── .opencode/skills/corgispec-*/     # ★ Source of Truth for skills
44	│   ├── SKILL.md                      #   AI-readable step instructions
45	│   ├── skill.meta.json               #   Machine-readable metadata
46	│   └── references/                   #   Supplementary references
47	├── .claude/skills/corgispec-*/       # Claude Code skill mirrors (must sync)
48	├── .codex/skills/corgispec-*/        # Codex skill mirrors (must sync)
49	├── .opencode/commands/corgi-*.md     # OpenCode slash command dispatch
50	├── .claude/commands/corgi/*.md       # Claude slash command dispatch
51	├── packages/corgispec/               # ★ Unified CLI (replaces ds-skills + install-skills.sh)
52	│   ├── src/bin/corgispec.ts          #   Commander.js entry point
53	│   ├── src/commands/                 #   14 subcommands
54	│   ├── src/lib/                      #   Reusable modules
55	│   ├── assets/                       #   Bundled resources
56	│   └── test/                         #   Vitest tests (114 tests)
57	├── tools/ds-skills/                  # Legacy CLI (being replaced by corgispec)
58	├── schemas/skill-meta.schema.json    # ★ JSON Schema for skill validation
59	├── openspec/
60	│   ├── config.yaml                   # Project config (schema, isolation, context, rules)
61	│   ├── schemas/{gitlab,github}-tracked/  # Schema definitions + templates
62	│   ├── specs/                        # Accumulated canonical specs
63	│   └── changes/                      # Active change directories
64	├── memory/                           # ★ Layer 1: Always-loaded session memory
65	│   ├── MEMORY.md                     #   Hard constraints (never expires)
66	│   ├── session-bridge.md             #   Last session handoff state
67	│   └── pitfalls.md                   #   Cross-change pitfall log
68	├── wiki/                             # ★ Layer 2: On-demand knowledge base
69	│   ├── hot.md                        #   Current project pulse (≤600 words)
70	│   ├── index.md                      #   Navigation index
71	│   └── architecture/ decisions/ patterns/ sessions/ ...
72	├── docs/                             # Layer 3: Traditional docs (untouched by memory)
73	├── README.md / README.zh-TW.md       # Bilingual documentation
74	└── AGENTS.md                         # Agent behavioral guidelines + memory protocol
75	```
76	
77	## 4. Skill Architecture
78	
79	### 4.1 Three-Tier Hierarchy (Atoms → Molecules → Compounds)
80	
81	| Tier | Description | Dependency Rule | Examples |
82	|------|-------------|----------------|----------|
83	| **Atom** | Single reusable operation | No dependencies | `corgispec-memory-init`, `corgispec-memory-extract` |
84	| **Molecule** | Workflow combining atoms | Atoms only | `corgispec-propose`, `corgispec-apply-change`, `corgispec-review`, `corgispec-verify` |
85	| **Compound** | End-to-end orchestration | Molecules only | (not yet implemented) |
86	
87	### 4.2 Complete Skill Inventory (17 skills)
88	
89	| Skill | Platform | Tier | Depends On |
90	|-------|----------|------|------------|
91	| `corgispec-memory-init` | universal | atom | — |
92	| `corgispec-memory-extract` | universal | atom | — |
93	| `corgispec-propose` | gitlab | molecule | — |
94	| `corgispec-apply-change` | gitlab | molecule | — |
95	| `corgispec-review` | gitlab | molecule | — |
96	| `corgispec-verify` | universal | molecule | — |
97	| `corgispec-archive-change` | gitlab | molecule | memory-extract |
98	| `corgispec-explore` | gitlab | molecule | — |
99	| `corgispec-install` | universal | molecule | memory-init |
100	| `corgispec-lint` | universal | molecule | memory-init |
101	| `corgispec-ask` | universal | molecule | memory-init |
102	| `corgispec-memory-migrate` | universal | molecule | memory-init |
103	| `corgispec-gh-propose` | github | molecule | — |
104	| `corgispec-gh-apply` | github | molecule | — |
105	| `corgispec-gh-review` | github | molecule | — |
106	| `corgispec-gh-archive` | github | molecule | — |
107	| `corgispec-gh-explore` | github | molecule | — |
108	
109	### 4.3 Platform Mapping Strategy
110	
111	- **GitLab**: `corgispec-*` (unprefixed — default skills using `glab` CLI)
112	- **GitHub**: `corgispec-gh-*` (`gh` prefixed variants using `gh` CLI)
113	- **Universal**: `corgispec-memory-*`, `corgispec-verify`, `corgispec-install`, `corgispec-lint`, `corgispec-ask` — no issue tracker dependency
114	
115	### 4.4 Skill Anatomy
116	
117	Each skill directory contains exactly two files:
118	
119	- **`SKILL.md`** — YAML frontmatter + numbered step-by-step AI instructions with Preconditions, Forbidden Actions, Steps, Output, and Postconditions
120	- **`skill.meta.json`** — Validated against `schemas/skill-meta.schema.json`; fields: `slug`, `tier`, `version`, `description`, `depends_on`, `platform`, `tags`, `installation`
121	
122	## 5. Schema Design (Artifact Pipeline)
123	
124	Both `gitlab-tracked` and `github-tracked` schemas produce the same 4-artifact pipeline:
125	
126	```
127	proposal.md → specs/<capability>/spec.md → design.md → tasks.md
128	```
129	
130	### Artifact Definitions
131	
132	| Artifact | File | Requires | Purpose |
133	|----------|------|----------|---------|
134	| proposal | `proposal.md` | — | Why, what changes, capabilities, impact |
135	| specs | `specs/**/*.md` | proposal | Formal requirements with WHEN/THEN scenarios |
136	| design | `design.md` | proposal | Technical decisions, architecture, risks |
137	| tasks | `tasks.md` | specs, design | Numbered Task Groups with checkboxes |
138	
139	### Key Schema Features
140	
141	- **Capabilities-driven specs**: Each capability from the proposal becomes `specs/<capability>/spec.md`
142	- **Delta operations**: ADDED / MODIFIED / REMOVED / RENAMED — changes accumulate into `openspec/specs/` at archive
143	- **Task Groups as checkpoints**: `## N. Group Name` in `tasks.md` = one child issue = one apply session = one review cycle
144	- **Template + instruction model**: Each artifact has a template (structure) and instruction (AI guidance); context/rules are constraints never included in output
145	
146	## 6. Cross-Session Memory System
147	
148	```
149	Layer 1: memory/ (always loaded at startup, ≤3 files, ≤2900 tokens)
150	    ├── MEMORY.md          → Hard constraints (project identity, sync rules, preferences)
151	    ├── session-bridge.md  → Last session Done / Waiting / Pitfalls / Discoveries (≤50 lines)
152	    └── pitfalls.md        → Cross-change pitfall log with source links (≤20 active entries)
153	
154	Layer 2: wiki/ (on-demand, wikilink-navigable)
155	    ├── hot.md             → Current project pulse (~500 words, hard cap 600)
156	    ├── index.md           → Navigation index (≤40 lines, hard cap 80)
157	    └── architecture/ decisions/ patterns/ sessions/ research/ questions/ meta/
158	
159	Layer 3: docs/ (untouched by memory system)
160	    └── Traditional docs, design specs, published articles
161	```
162	
163	### Self-Maintenance Rules
164	
165	- **Startup**: Read session-bridge → hot → index (max 3 files), then on-demand
166	- **Per-question**: Max 2 wiki pages before answering
167	- **After each Task Group complete**: Append pitfalls → `memory/pitfalls.md`; update decisions → `wiki/hot.md`
168	- **Every archive**: Compress session-bridge
169	- **pitfalls > 20 entries**: Rotate oldest 10 to Archive
170	- **hot.md > 550 words**: Trim oldest entries
171	- **Every 10 corgi sessions**: Suggest running `/corgi-lint`
172	- **11 lint checks**: Freshness, size caps, broken links, extraction completeness
173	
174	## 7. CLI Architecture (corgispec)
175	
176	`packages/corgispec/` — TypeScript, Commander.js, built with tsup, tested with Vitest (114 tests).
177	
178	### Command Map (14 subcommands)
179	
180	| Category | Command | Responsibility |
181	|----------|---------|---------------|
182	| **Setup** | `bootstrap` | Single-entry installer (fetch + clone + build + init) |
183	| | `install` | Project-local asset install (fresh/update/legacy/verify) |
184	| | `init` | Initialize openspec config |
185	| | `doctor` | Environment diagnostics |
186	| **Skill Mgmt** | `validate` | Schema + constraint validation of all skills |
187	| | `list` | List skills with tier/platform filters |
188	| | `graph` | Dependency graph (mermaid/dot output) |
189	| **Workflow** | `status` | Query change status |
190	| | `instructions` | Generate artifact instructions for AI |
191	| | `propose` | Create change directory |
192	| | `apply` | Trigger apply phase |
193	| | `review` | Trigger review phase |
194	| | `archive` | Trigger archive phase |
195	
196	### Lib Module Reuse
197	
198	| Module | Responsibility |
199	|--------|---------------|
200	| `config.ts` | Read/parse `openspec/config.yaml` |
201	| `skills.ts` | Skill discovery, parsing, validation |
202	| `schemas.ts` | Schema loading, artifact pipeline |
203	| `platform.ts` | Platform detection (gitlab vs github) |
204	| `bootstrap.ts` | Bootstrap orchestration logic |
205	| `install-assets.ts` | Asset copy/sync with manifest tracking |
206	| `memory-init.ts` | Memory system initialization |
207	| `changes.ts` | Change directory management |
208	
209	## 8. Key Design Principles
210	
211	1. **Agent-first**: All workflows triggered via LLM slash commands, not manual CLI
212	2. **Skill as product**: Core deliverable is SKILL.md files, not executable code
213	3. **Three-directory sync obligation**: `.opencode/` (canonical), `.claude/`, `.codex/` must stay in sync
214	4. **Checkpoint discipline**: Each Task Group is an atomic checkpoint with forced pause-for-review
215	5. **Memory-first continuity**: 3-layer memory solves AI session statelessness
216	6. **Platform abstraction**: Skill files use generic tool names; platform mapping at runtime
217	7. **Legacy coexistence**: `ds-skills` + `install-skills.sh` are legacy; `corgispec` CLI is the future
218	8. **No CI**: Validation is manual via `corgispec validate` or `ds-skills validate`
219	
220	## 9. Compared to Vanilla OpenSpec
221	
222	| Capability | Vanilla OpenSpec | This Project |
223	|------------|-----------------|-------------|
224	| Issue tracking | None | Parent/child via `glab`/`gh` CLI |
225	| Apply behavior | All tasks at once | Checkpoint-based, one group at a time |
226	| Progress sync | Local checkboxes only | Rich summaries posted to issues |
227	| Workflow labels | None | `backlog → todo → in-progress → review → done` |
228	| Review | None | 5-axis + verify gate + approve/reject/discuss |
229	| Spec format | Generic | Delta operations + formal scenarios |
230	| Worktree isolation | None | Opt-in `git worktree` parallel dev |
231	| Cross-session memory | None | 3-layer with ≤2900 token startup |
232	| Skill architecture | Flat files | Composable 3-tier with schema validation |
233	
234	## 10. Recent Activity
235	
236	- **Active change**: `single-entry bootstrap install` (branch: `bootstrap-install`) — implementation complete, branch preserved
237	- **`corgispec bootstrap`** now covers: fresh install, managed update, legacy migration, verify-only, user-level skill sync, project memory init, machine-readable JSON output
238	- **114 tests passing** in `packages/corgispec/` as of 2026-05-02

30	## Steps

32	### 1. Discover: Select change and resolve worktree
33	
34	Read `openspec/config.yaml` for `isolation` settings.

36	**If `isolation.mode: worktree`**: Changes live inside worktrees, not the main checkout. Read `references/worktree-discovery.md` for the full discovery procedure. Quick summary:

51	```

53	Handle states: `blocked` → stop. `all_done` → suggest review/archive. Otherwise → proceed.
54	
55	Read all files listed in `contextFiles`.
56	
57	### 3. Discover: Parse Task Groups, find current group, and plan delegation
58	
59	Read `references/checkpoint-flow.md` for: Task Group parsing from `tasks.md`, identifying the current group (first group with pending tasks), and building the group progress table.
60	
61	Read `references/delegation-strategy.md` for: how to analyze tasks within the current group, decide which to delegate to subagents, and how to structure delegation prompts.
62	
63	**Quick summary**: Analyze task dependencies within the group. Independent tasks SHOULD be delegated to subagents for cleaner context and potential parallelism. Each subagent gets only the relevant context files and task description — not the full conversation history.
64

71	   - Show which task is being worked on
72	   - Make minimal, focused code changes
73	   - Mark each task complete in `tasks.md`: `- [ ]` → `- [x]`
74	   - Record the files actually created or modified for closeout
75	   - Continue until the group is done or a blocker is hit
76	c. On blocker: report and wait for guidance.

78	### 5. Closeout: Generate summary, sync, and prepare review handoff
79	
80	After all tasks in the group are complete, close out the group without doing new implementation work:
81	
82	1. Generate rich summary (read `references/issue-sync.md` for format)
83	2. Sync to issue tracker if tracked (child issue description + completion note + labels)
84	3. Update parent issue progress if tracked

90	   - Update `wiki/hot.md` Recent Decisions if significant decisions were made

95	If implementation work is complete but closeout fails, retry closeout and sync rather than re-running the group implementation.

103	**Progress:** A/B tasks complete

58	
59	Run automated quality checks and assemble a Review Report.

19	- Costs ≤ 3000 tokens at session startup
20	- Is human-readable in Obsidian (valid markdown with wikilinks)
21	- Self-compacts via size caps and rotation rules
22	- Integrates with the OpenSpec lifecycle (apply closeout, archive extraction)
23	
24	## When to Use
25	
26	- After `corgispec-install` completes (called automatically unless `--no-memory`)
27	- Manually via `/corgi-memory-init` to add memory to an existing OpenSpec project
28	- When a project needs cross-session continuity but doesn't have `memory/` or `wiki/` yet
29	
30	Do not use this skill to modify memory contents, run lint checks, or extract knowledge from changes.
31	
32	## Preconditions
33	
34	- [ ] Target project has `openspec/config.yaml`
35	- [ ] Target project root is known (current working directory or specified path)
36

44	2. If `CLAUDE.md` exists → extract:
45	   - Tech stack (languages, frameworks, databases, infrastructure)
46	   - Hard constraints (lines with "must", "never", "always", "required", "forbidden")
47	   - Preferences (style, naming conventions, patterns to follow)
48	   - Architecture notes (component descriptions, service boundaries)
49	3. If `AGENTS.md` exists → extract same categories as CLAUDE.md (merge, deduplicate)
50	4. If `GEMINI.md` exists → extract same categories (merge, deduplicate)
51	5. If `package.json` exists → extract project name from `name` field, framework info from dependencies
52	6. If `docs/` exists → scan top-level .md filenames for architecture clues (don't deep-read, just note presence)

55	- `PROJECT_NAME`: from README heading or package.json name

57	- `TECH_STACK`: from agent configs + package.json deps

60	- `STABLE_COMPONENTS`: well-established tech from stack info
61	- `EVOLVING_COMPONENTS`: anything described as new/experimental, or "TBD"

384	### 4. Inject Session Memory Protocol
385	
386	Check the agent configuration file for an existing `## Session Memory Protocol` section:
387	
388	1. If `CLAUDE.md` exists → check for `## Session Memory Protocol`
389	2. If `AGENTS.md` exists → check for `## Session Memory Protocol`
390

392	
393	**If the section does NOT exist** → append the following block to the end of the primary config file (CLAUDE.md if it exists, otherwise AGENTS.md):
394	
395	```markdown
396	## Session Memory Protocol
397	
398	### Startup (every session)
399	Read in order, max 3 files:
400	1. `memory/session-bridge.md` — last session's state
401	2. `wiki/hot.md` — current project context (~500 words, hard cap 600)
402	3. `wiki/index.md` — jump to relevant domain page
403	Then read `docs/` or code as needed.
404	
405	### Retrieval Budget
406	- Startup: max 3 files (session-bridge + hot + index), then on-demand
407	- Per-question: max 2 wiki pages before answering
408	- If >5 pages needed: say "this needs a deep session"
409	
410	### File Size Limits (hard caps)
411	| File | Target | Hard Cap | Overflow Action |
412	|------|--------|----------|-----------------|
413	| wiki/hot.md | 500 words | 600 words | Trim oldest entries |
414	| wiki/index.md | 40 lines | 80 lines | Archive completed entries |
415	| memory/pitfalls.md | 10 active | 20 active | Rotate oldest 10 |
416	| memory/session-bridge.md | 30 lines | 50 lines | Archive old Done items |
417	
418	### Shutdown (every session end)
419	Update `memory/session-bridge.md`: Done / Waiting / New Pitfalls / New Discoveries
420	
421	### opsx Apply → Long-term Memory
422	After each Task Group completes:
423	- New pitfalls → append to `memory/pitfalls.md` (link source change)
424	- New implicit rules → append to `wiki/architecture/implicit-contracts.md`
425	- Update `wiki/hot.md` Recent Decisions
426	
427	### Compaction Triggers (agent self-maintains)
428	- Every archive: compress session-bridge
429	- pitfalls > 20 entries: rotate oldest 10 to Archive section
430	- hot.md > 550 words: trim oldest entries
431	- Every 10 opsx sessions: suggest running /corgi-lint
432	```
433	
434	### 5. Idempotency and safety
435	
436	Throughout all steps, enforce these rules:
437	
438	- **Never overwrite existing files.** If a file already exists at the target path, skip it and add to the "skipped" report.
439	- **Never overwrite existing protocol section.** If `## Session Memory Protocol` already exists in the config file, do not duplicate it.
440	- **Only operate within the project directory.** Never write to user-home or global paths.
441	- **Report all actions.** At the end, print a summary:
442	  - Files created (list paths)
443	  - Files skipped (already existed)
444	  - Protocol injection status (injected / already present / no config file found)
445	  - Next steps recommendation
446	
447	### 6. Output summary
448	
449	Print a structured summary:
450	
451	```
452	## Memory Init Complete
453	
454	**Project**: <name>
455	**Created**: N files
456	**Skipped**: M files (already existed)
457	**Protocol**: Injected into <file> / Already present / No config file

461	- memory/session-bridge.md
462	- memory/pitfalls.md
463	- wiki/hot.md
464	- wiki/index.md
465	- wiki/architecture/_index.md
466	- wiki/architecture/implicit-contracts.md
467	- wiki/patterns/_index.md

469	- wiki/sessions/_index.md
470	- wiki/decisions/_index.md

472	- wiki/meta/_index.md
473	
474	### Next Steps
475	1. Review `memory/MEMORY.md` — fill in any placeholders
476	2. Run `/corgi-propose` to create your first change
477	3. Memory will auto-update during apply and archive phases
478	```

481	
482	- Overwriting existing memory files (violates idempotency)
483	- Injecting Session Memory Protocol twice
484	- Using absolute paths instead of project-relative wikilinks
485	- Forgetting to extract project identity before writing templates

3	# Cross-Session Memory
4	
5	AI coding sessions are stateless by default — every new session starts from scratch. OpenSpec GitFlow adds a **3-layer memory system** that gives AI agents cross-session continuity while staying under a strict token budget (~2900 tokens at startup).
6	
7	## The Problem
8	
9	Without memory, each session wastes tokens re-discovering:
10	- What was decided in prior sessions
11	- What pitfalls were already hit (and hit again)
12	- What the project's implicit rules and contracts are
13	- Where the last session left off
14	
15	## Architecture
16

19	    classDef layer1 fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
20	    classDef layer2 fill:#16213e,stroke:#0f3460,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px

22	    classDef trigger fill:#533483,stroke:#533483,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px

81	
82	## Lifecycle Integration
83	
84	```mermaid
85	sequenceDiagram
86	    participant H as Human
87	    participant A as AI Agent
88	    participant M as memory/
89	    participant W as wiki/
90	    participant D as docs/
91	
92	    Note over A,M: ── Session Startup ──
93	    A->>M: Read session-bridge.md
94	    A->>W: Read hot.md + index.md
95	    Note right of A: Total: ~2900 tokens
96	
97	    Note over H,A: ── Development ──
98	    H->>A: /corgi-apply
99	    A->>D: Read code & docs as needed
100	    A->>W: Read ≤2 wiki pages per question
101	
102	    Note over A,W: ── Apply Closeout ──
103	    A->>M: Update session-bridge (Done/Waiting)
104	    A->>M: Append new pitfalls
105	    A->>W: Update hot.md decisions
106	
107	    Note over A,W: ── Archive ──
108	    A->>W: Extract patterns → wiki/patterns/
109	    A->>W: Create session summary → wiki/sessions/
110	    A->>M: Reset session-bridge
111	    A->>W: Move change to Recently Shipped
112	
113	    Note over A,M: ── Session Shutdown ──
114	    A->>M: Final session-bridge update
115	```
116	
117	## File Structure
118	
119	```
120	Layer 1: memory/          ← Always loaded at startup (~1300 tokens)
121	├── MEMORY.md             Hard constraints, project identity (write-once)
122	├── session-bridge.md     Last session's handoff state (write-every-session)
123	└── pitfalls.md           Cross-change pitfall log (append + rotate)
124	
125	Layer 2: wiki/            ← On-demand, navigated via index (~700 tokens for index)
126	├── hot.md                Project pulse, recent decisions (write-every-session)
127	├── index.md              Navigation hub with wikilinks (80-line cap)
128	├── architecture/         Structural insights, implicit contracts
129	├── patterns/             Reusable approaches from completed changes
130	├── sessions/             Historical summaries of archived changes
131	├── decisions/            Key decisions from reviews
132	├── research/             Investigation results from explore sessions
133	└── questions/            Human Q&A via Obsidian
134	
135	Layer 3: docs/            ← Existing project documentation (untouched)
136	```
137	
138	## Key Design Decisions
139	
140	| Decision | Rationale |
141	|----------|-----------|
142	| Word/line caps instead of token counting | Skills have no runtime — AI self-enforces during writes |
143	| Separate `memory/` from `wiki/` | Core memory (always loaded) vs archival (on-demand) — from MemGPT research |
144	| Compaction as agent self-maintenance | No cron/daemon available — agent compacts during normal writes |
145	| Lint as standalone, not gate | Never blocks workflow; periodic health check via `/corgi-lint` |
146	| Early-stop retrieval for ask | Budget-aware: max 2 wiki pages per question, stop when sufficient |
147	
148	## File Size Limits
149	
150	Memory self-compacts via hard caps. The AI agent enforces these during writes; `/corgi-lint` validates post-hoc:
151	
152	| File | Target | Hard Cap | Overflow Action |
153	|------|--------|----------|-----------------|
154	| `wiki/hot.md` | 500 words | 600 words | Trim oldest entries |
155	| `wiki/index.md` | 40 lines | 80 lines | Archive completed entries |
156	| `memory/pitfalls.md` | 10 active | 20 active | Rotate oldest 10 to Archive |
157	| `memory/session-bridge.md` | 30 lines | 50 lines | Archive old Done items |
158	
159	## Getting Started
160	
161	### New Project
162	
163	Memory initializes automatically during `/corgi-install` (opt-out with `--no-memory`):
164	
165	```text
166	/corgi-install --path /path/to/project
167	# Installer asks: "Initialize memory structure? (yes/no — default: yes)"
168	```
169	
170	### Existing Project (no prior knowledge)
171	
172	Add memory to a project that already uses OpenSpec:
173	
174	```text
175	/corgi-memory-init
176	```
177	
178	### Existing Project with Accumulated Knowledge
179	
180	Migrate docs, archived changes, and vault pages into the memory structure:
181	
182	```text
183	/corgi-migrate
184	```
185	
186	The migrate skill runs 4 phases:
187	
188	1. **Agent Config Deepening** (auto) — extracts constraints from CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md into `memory/MEMORY.md`
189	2. **Archived Changes** (auto) — generates session summaries and patterns from `openspec/changes/archive/`
190	3. **docs/ Directory** (hybrid) — auto-categorizes obvious docs, asks about ambiguous ones
191	4. **Vault Pages** (hybrid) — presents found .md files for user categorization
192	
193	Migration never moves or deletes source files — it creates wiki entries that reference originals.
194	
195	## Obsidian Compatibility
196	
197	All memory files are valid markdown with `[[wikilinks]]`. If your project is also an Obsidian vault:
198	- `wiki/` renders as a navigable knowledge graph
199	- `memory/` shows session state at a glance
200	- Humans can browse, search, and even edit memory files directly

1	---
2	name: corgispec-verify
3	description: Automated verification gate between apply and review — runs tests, checks spec coverage, validates lint/build. No human gate required.
4	license: MIT
5	compatibility: Requires openspec CLI.
6	metadata:
7	  author: openspec
8	  version: "1.0"
9	  generatedBy: "1.3.0"
10	---
11	
12	Run automated verification on a completed Task Group before review. Verify assures the code works; review judges if it's good enough.
13	
14	## Preconditions (VERIFY BEFORE STARTING)
15	
16	- [ ] Change exists in `openspec/changes/<name>/`
17	- [ ] If tracked: `.gitlab.yaml` or `.github.yaml` exists with group issue numbers
18	- [ ] If `isolation.mode` is `worktree`: worktree exists for this change (error if not)
19	- [ ] At least one Task Group has all tasks complete (verify needs something to check)
20	
21	## Forbidden Actions
22	
23	- NEVER ask for human input — verify is fully automated
24	- NEVER implement fixes — flag issues, do not resolve them
25	- NEVER create or close issues — only post a report note to existing child issues
26	- NEVER change issue labels or workflow state
27	- NEVER fabricate test results or evidence
28	- NEVER add Playwright/UI screenshots (those belong in review, not verify)
29	
30	---
31	
32	## Steps
33	
34	### 1. Discover: select change and resolve worktree
35	
36	Read `openspec/config.yaml` for schema (gitlab-tracked / github-tracked) and `isolation` settings.
37	
38	**If `isolation.mode: worktree`**: Changes live inside worktrees. Read `references/worktree-discovery.md` from the review skill for the full discovery procedure (this is a dependency of verify). Quick summary:
39	1. `openspec list --json`, if it returns changes, use them
40	2. If empty: scan `<isolation.root>/` directories, verify each with `git worktree list` and check `openspec/changes/<name>/` exists inside
41	3. Auto-select if one found, prompt if multiple
42	4. ALL subsequent work uses the worktree as workdir
43	
44	**If no isolation**: `openspec list --json` directly. Auto-select if one, prompt if multiple.
45	
46	If name provided by user, use it directly.
47	
48	### 2. Discover: select completed Task Group
49	
50	Parse `tasks.md` to find all Task Groups (`## N.` headings). Identify which groups have ALL tasks marked `[x]`. These are the completed groups eligible for verification.
51	
52	If tracked: read the tracking file (`.gitlab.yaml` / `.github.yaml`) for group names and issue numbers.
53	
54	Default to the first completed group that hasn't been verified yet. If no completed groups exist, guidance: "No completed groups found. Run `/corgi-apply` first."
55	
56	### 3. Automated Test Execution
57	
58	Detect the project's test infrastructure and run the test suite. All detection is best-effort — skip gracefully if nothing is found.
59	
60	Use the detection table in `references/verification-steps.md` for detailed detection conditions and commands. Summary:
61	
62	| Detection Condition | Run Command | Failure Handling |
63	|---------------------|-------------|-----------------|
64	| `tests/` + `pytest.ini` / `pyproject.toml` with `[tool.pytest]` | `python -m pytest -v` | If tests fail → mark 🟡 Important, do NOT block |
65	| `package.json` with `"test"` script | `npm test` | Same as above |
66	| `bun test` exists or `bunfig.toml` | `bun test` | Same as above |
67	| `go.mod` + `_test.go` files | `go test ./...` | Same as above |
68	| No test infrastructure | Report "No test infrastructure detected", mark ⚠️, do not block |
69	
70	**Test result handling**:
71	- All tests pass → ✅ output captured, continue
72	- Some tests fail → capture failure output, mark 🟡 Important, continue
73	- No test infrastructure → mark ⚠️, note in report, continue
74	
75	Tests are evidence. Failure is a signal, not a gate.
76	
77	### 4. Spec Coverage Verification
78	
79	Compare the change's spec requirements against the actual implementation.
80	
81	Steps:
82	1. Read the spec file(s) from `openspec/changes/<name>/specs/<capability>/spec.md`
83	2. If no spec exists: note "No spec found for this group", mark ℹ️, skip
84	3. For each Requirement in the spec, check:
85	   - Is there corresponding implementation code that fulfills this requirement?
86	   - Do the Scenario behaviors map to actual code paths?
87	4. Classify each requirement:
88	   - **✅ Full**: Implementation covers all scenarios with evidence (specific file:function references)
89	   - **⚠️ Partial**: Some scenarios covered, some missing or untestable
90	   - **❌ Missing**: No implementation found for this requirement
91	
92	Output format follows the spec in `references/verification-steps.md`.
93	
94	### 5. Lint / Build Verification
95	
96	Check for project-level lint and build configuration. Run what's available.
97	
98	**Lint detection**:
99	| Condition | Command |
100	|-----------|---------|
101	| `.ruff.toml` or `[tool.ruff]` in `pyproject.toml` | `ruff check` |
102	| `.eslintrc.*` or `eslint` in `package.json` | `npx eslint .` (or configured path) |
103	| `tsconfig.json` | `npx tsc --noEmit` |
104	| No linter found | Skip, mark ℹ️ |
105	
106	**Build detection**:
107	| Condition | Command |
108	|-----------|---------|
109	| `package.json` with `"build"` script | `npm run build` |
110	| `Makefile` with `build` target | `make build` |
111	| No build config | Skip, mark ℹ️ |
112	
113	**Result handling**:
114	- Lint/build passes → ✅ captured, continue
115	- Lint/build fails → capture output, mark 🟡 Important, continue
116	- No config found → mark ℹ️, note, continue
117	
118	### 6. Generate Verify Report
119	
120	Assemble all verification results into a structured report. Read `references/verification-steps.md` for detailed report composition guidance.
121	
122	Report format (for terminal display and issue posting):
123	
124	```markdown
125	## Verify Report: Group N, {group name}
126	
127	### Test Results
128	✅ 12 passed / ❌ 0 failed / ⚠️ 2 skipped
129	
130	<test output or "No test infrastructure detected">
131	
132	### Spec Coverage
133	| Requirement | Coverage | Evidence |
134	|-------------|----------|----------|
135	| REQ-1: User login | ✅ Full | Implemented in auth/login.py:login() |
136	| REQ-2: Error handling | ⚠️ Partial | Happy path covered, null input not handled |
137	| REQ-3: Rate limiting | ❌ Missing | No rate limiting found in codebase |
138	
139	**Summary**: ✅ 2/3 fully covered | ⚠️ 1 partial | ❌ 0 missing
140	
141	### Lint / Build
142	✅ ruff: no errors | ✅ Build: success
143	
144	### Verdict
145	✅ **PASS** — Ready for review
146	```
147	
148	#### Verdict Determination
149	
150	- **✅ PASS**: All detectable checks passed (tests pass, spec fully covered, lint clean, build successful)
151	- **⚠️ PASS WITH WARNINGS**: Some checks have warnings (partial spec coverage, test failures, lint warnings) — not blocking, but noted
152	- **❌ FAIL**: Critical issues found that MUST be fixed before review (e.g., build broken, zero spec coverage for core requirements, security red flags from security checklist)
153	
154	### 7. Gate Decision and Next-Steps Guidance
155	
156	Based on the verdict from Step 6:
157	
158	**If ✅ PASS or ⚠️ PASS WITH WARNINGS**:
159	- Post the verify report to the child issue (if tracked):
160	  - GitLab: `glab issue note <child_iid> --message "$VERIFY_REPORT"`
161	  - GitHub: `gh issue comment <child_number> --body "$VERIFY_REPORT"`
162	- Print guidance: `Run /corgi-review to review this group.`
163	- If warnings present, also print: `N warnings were found. They are noted in the report but do not block review.`
164	
165	**If ❌ FAIL**:
166	- Post the verify report to the child issue (if tracked)
167	- Print guidance: `N critical issues found. Fix these before review. Run /corgi-apply to address them.`
168	- List the specific failure items for the user
169	
170	**Verify cannot replace review**. Verify proves the code works. Review judges if the code is good enough. The human gate in review is always required after verify passes.
171	
172	## Guardrails
173	
174	- Verify is fully automated — no human gate, no confirmation dialogs
175	- Evidence gathering is best-effort — skip gracefully if detection fails
176	- Test/lint/build failures are signals, not gates (except build failure = FAIL)
177	- Verify never implements fixes, changes labels, or creates/close issues
178	- Verify posts only a report note to the existing child issue (if tracked)
179	- Platform detection: read `openspec/config.yaml` schema field, use `glab` for gitlab-tracked, `gh` for github-tracked
180	- If no tracking file exists: produce the report to terminal only, no issue posting
181	- If no completed groups found: stop with guidance, do not proceed
182	
183	## Postconditions (VERIFY BEFORE REPORTING DONE)
184	
185	- [ ] Change discovered and selected
186	- [ ] Completed Task Group identified
187	- [ ] Tests executed (or gracefully skipped with note)
188	- [ ] Spec coverage assessed (or gracefully skipped if no spec)
189	- [ ] Lint/build checked (or gracefully skipped if no config)
190	- [ ] Verify report generated with explicit verdict
191	- [ ] Report posted to child issue (if tracked)
192	- [ ] Next-steps guidance printed for user
193	
194	**If you reached postconditions without producing an explicit PASS/PASS WITH WARNINGS/FAIL verdict, you violated the contract. Stop and determine the verdict.**
195	

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

47
